summary: Before I enlisted, I was working a civil service job at the Brooklyn Navy Yard in New York. I saw how the sailors there always got their food and worked on clean ships, which inspired me to join the Navy and Coast Guard. Living in Brooklyn at the time, it was a familiar place that connected me to the maritime service.
cues: inspiration to join Navy and Coast Guard

summary: I served in the United States Coast Guard starting in July 1942 and was a Storekeeper on the USS Bisbee. My duties involved managing supplies and payroll on the ship, and I was proud to be recognized with a Good Conduct Medal. I also met my future wife Joan while we were both serving at Coast Guard headquarters in Washington, D.C.

I served during World War II from 1942 until 1946, participating in significant battles like Leyte Gulf in the Philippines. Although the war ended in August 1945, I stayed in service until my enlistment ended. The war shaped much of my life, including the friendships I made and the experiences I had in the Pacific theater.

Towards the end of my service, we were stationed at Attu Island in the Aleutians, Alaska. After our time there, we turned our ship over to the Russians as part of a lend-lease agreement. Attu was one of the last places I served before being transferred to Coast Guard headquarters.

The lend-lease plan was how the US provided ships and material to the Russians during and after World War II. Our ship, the USS Bisbee, was turned over to the Russians under this agreement after we left Attu Island in 1946. It was a gesture to support the Russians who had suffered heavy losses in the war.

I was awarded a medal for service in the American Theater, which covered operations around the US Atlantic and Pacific coasts. Most of the servicemen received this medal, and I was proud to have it as part of my military decorations. It recognized the important, though sometimes less visible, work done close to home.

I also received a medal for the Asiatic-Pacific campaign, reflecting my service in the Pacific during World War II. I was involved in battles near the Philippines and New Guinea, witnessing intense combat like the battle of Leyte Gulf. This theater was where much of my wartime experience took place.

I was awarded the Good Conduct Medal because I stayed out of trouble and did my duties well during my four years in the Coast Guard. It was a nice recognition for being a 'good boy' in the service. I kept the medal as a reminder of my time and conduct in the military.

My role in the Coast Guard was Storekeeper, where I managed all supplies and payroll on the ship. I was responsible for making sure the crew got paid on time, which made me well-liked among the men. It was an important job that kept the ship running smoothly.

One of my most memorable experiences was at Leyte Gulf in the Philippines during a major naval battle. We were about 10 miles from the fighting and watched the massive exchange of shells and aircraft attacks. It was like watching a movie scene, but very real and intense.

I served aboard the USS Bisbee, a patrol frigate designated PF-46, during World War II. The ship saw action in the Pacific, including the Leyte Gulf battle, and later was turned over to the Russians under lend-lease. I was proud to be part of the crew and to serve as the Storekeeper on that vessel.

After the war ended, we stopped in Seattle for a week or two before I was transferred to Coast Guard headquarters. It was there that I was promoted to chief storekeeper and met Joan, who worked in cryptography. Seattle was a brief but important stop in my post-combat service.

I was transferred to Coast Guard headquarters in Washington, D.C. after my time at sea. There I worked alongside Joan, who was involved in cryptography using IBM machines. It was a different pace from ship life, and it was where our paths crossed during the war.

Joan worked with IBM machines at the naval office as part of cryptography efforts during the war. These machines were used to send codes to ships at sea, and I learned about this when I met her at Coast Guard headquarters. It was fascinating technology for that time.

I spent part of my service in Washington, D.C. at Coast Guard headquarters after returning from the Pacific. It was there I met Joan, who was also serving in the military. I was discharged in June 1946, and Washington was the place where my service was winding down.

After World War II, we turned over our ship, the USS Bisbee, to Russia under the lend-lease agreement. The Russians used the ship for several years, even during the Korean War period. This transfer was part of the US effort to support Russia after their heavy losses in the war.

The USS Bisbee was eventually returned by Russia in Yokosuka, Japan, around 1950 during the Korean War. This was after the ship had been in Russian hands for several years following World War II. Yokosuka was a key naval base where the ship's status was noted.

The Gallup, designated PF-47, was a sister ship to the USS Bisbee and was also part of the lend-lease transfer to Russia. I recall reading about the Gallup in an article alongside the Bisbee, showing how these ships were given to Russia after the war. They continued to be relevant during the Korean War period.

Though I was discharged before the Korean War began, I learned that the ships we gave to Russia, like the USS Bisbee, were still in use during that conflict around 1950. It was interesting to see how the equipment from World War II had a role in later wars. My service ended in 1946, so I was not involved directly.

I saw combat starting around New Guinea, which is a large island north of Australia. We operated in that region and nearby islands for about seven months before moving on to the Philippines. It was a significant part of my wartime experience in the Pacific.

Australia was near the areas where we operated during the war, especially around New Guinea and the islands leading to the Philippines. I remember Australian soldiers coming aboard our ship after patrols, sometimes bringing back evidence of combat. It was part of the broader Allied effort in the Pacific.

I didn't know much about the Holocaust while I was in service; we had limited news and no TV like today. It was only after I got home that I learned about the full horror of what happened. Of course, I was horrified when I found out, like many others.

The attack on Pearl Harbor happened in December 1941, about seven months before I enlisted in July 1942. I remember the date well because it marked the US entry into the war. Although I wasn't yet in service, it was a defining moment that influenced many of us to join.
cues: US entry into World War II
